name: Mount Holyoke Seminary tracksite, South Hadley
short_description: A historic fossil tracksite on the Mount Holyoke Seminary campus featuring early Jurassic dinosaur footprints and trace fossils embedded in Triassic red sandstone.
description:
Mount Holyoke Seminary tracksite preserves one of the most important collections of early Jurassic vertebrate trace fossils in North America. Located on the college campus in South Hadley, this site contains thousands of dinosaur footprints, primarily from small theropods and archosaurs that inhabited the rift valley of the Connecticut Valley during the Triassic and early Jurassic periods, approximately 200 million years ago.

The tracksite is embedded within the Portland Formation, a sequence of fluvial red sandstones, siltstones, and mudstones deposited in a shallow lake and river system. The fine-grained nature of these sediments allowed for exceptional preservation of delicate footprints and tail drags. Visitors can observe cross-bedding, ripple marks, and other sedimentary structures that reveal the ancient depositional environment.

This is primarily a scientific study site rather than a traditional rockhounding location. Access is typically restricted and guided by Mount Holyoke College staff or paleontology researchers. The site is of exceptional educational and paleontological value and should be treated with great respect. Photography and casual observation may be possible during scheduled tours or with permission, but collecting is strictly prohibited.

The geology of the Connecticut Valley rift basin is crucial to understanding early dinosaur evolution. The trackways preserved here provide evidence of behavior, locomotion, and species diversity from a critical time period in the Mesozoic Era. This location has been studied by paleontologists for over 150 years.
history: The Mount Holyoke Seminary tracksite preserves fossils from the Triassic and early Jurassic periods, approximately 200 million years ago, when the Connecticut Valley rift basin formed during the breakup of the supercontinent Pangaea. The site's Triassic red sandstones were deposited in shallow lakes and rivers by the proto-Atlantic opening, and the exceptional preservation of vertebrate trace fossils here has made it one of the most scientifically significant paleontological sites in the eastern United States.
tips: This is a restricted educational site - contact Mount Holyoke College's geology or paleontology department to arrange access or guided tours. The best views of track-bearing surfaces are typically on exposed sandstone blocks and cliff faces on the campus grounds. Bring binoculars and a hand lens to examine fine details of fossil footprints, tail drags, and sedimentary structures without touching specimens. Focus your attention on the reddish-orange sandstone layers where most footprints are preserved, and note the varying sizes of trackways which represent different dinosaur species.
terrain_notes: The site sits at 196 feet elevation on a gentle slope within the Mount Holyoke Seminary campus in South Hadley. The terrain includes mixed woodland, manicured college grounds, and exposed sandstone outcrops. Access is limited to campus roads and designated areas. The tracksite itself features rocky, uneven fossil-bearing outcrops that require careful footing. Distance to main fossils varies depending on campus access permissions.
written_directions: From South Hadley town center, head north on College Street toward Mount Holyoke College's main campus entrance. Follow directional signs toward the geology department or visitor center. The tracksite is located on the seminary campus itself. Contact the college geology department in advance to arrange access, as the site is not open to casual visitors. Parking is available at designated college lots.
road_conditions: Paved college campus roads in good condition
parking_notes: Designated parking available at Mount Holyoke College campus lots. Visitor parking may require permits or registration at the main gate.
permit_link: Contact Mount Holyoke College Geology Department directly
collection_rules: This is a protected educational and research site on private college property. No collecting is permitted. Access is restricted to authorized visits arranged through Mount Holyoke College. Photography and study of fossils in place are subject to college approval. Collecting, excavation, or removal of any material is strictly prohibited. This is an active study site used by paleontologists and geology students.
rules:
Access by appointment only through Mount Holyoke College
No collecting of any fossils or rock samples
No excavation or disturbance of track-bearing surfaces
Photography and scientific study only with permission
Respect all campus regulations and restricted areas
Stay on designated paths and viewing areas
nearest_services: South Hadley town center, approximately 1-2 miles away, offers gas, restaurants, and shopping
accessibility_notes: The site is located on a college campus with paved roads and relatively flat terrain in most areas. However, the actual tracksite features rocky, uneven fossil-bearing outcrops that may be challenging for visitors with limited mobility. Campus buildings and pathways are generally accessible, but fossil study areas may involve uneven ground.

faq.question: What kind of fossils can you see at the Mount Holyoke Seminary tracksite?
faq.answer: The Mount Holyoke Seminary tracksite features thousands of dinosaur footprints and trace fossils from the Triassic and early Jurassic periods, approximately 200 million years ago. Most trackways were made by small theropod dinosaurs and archosaurs. The fossils are preserved in fine-grained red sandstone, which has allowed for exceptional preservation of footprints, tail drags, and other behavioral traces.

faq.question: Can you collect fossils at Mount Holyoke Seminary in South Hadley?
faq.answer: No, collecting is strictly prohibited at this site. Mount Holyoke Seminary tracksite is a protected educational and research facility on private college property. Access is limited to authorized visits arranged through Mount Holyoke College's geology department, and visitors may only observe and photograph fossils with special permission.

faq.question: How do you arrange a visit to the Mount Holyoke dinosaur tracksite?
faq.answer: Contact Mount Holyoke College's geology or paleontology department directly to request access. This is an active research site used by students and paleontologists, so visits must be pre-arranged. The college may offer guided tours during certain times of the year or as part of educational programs. Call ahead to determine availability.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit the Mount Holyoke tracksite?
faq.answer: The best visiting season is April through October, when weather is pleasant and campus facilities are in full operation. Spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures for studying the tracksite. Access may be limited during winter months or summer breaks when fewer college staff are on campus.

faq.question: What geological period do the fossils at Mount Holyoke represent?
faq.answer: The fossils at Mount Holyoke Seminary tracksite are from the Triassic and early Jurassic periods, approximately 200 million years ago. They preserve evidence of life in the Connecticut Valley rift basin, which formed during the breakup of Pangaea as the Atlantic Ocean was opening. This makes the site crucial to understanding early dinosaur evolution and behavior.

faq.question: Is the Mount Holyoke tracksite suitable for beginners and children?
faq.answer: Yes, if you arrange access through the college, the site is suitable for educational visits with children ages 8 and up. The campus terrain is generally accessible, and a guided tour by college paleontologists can provide excellent learning about dinosaur fossils and Connecticut Valley geology. However, the actual fossil-bearing outcrops have uneven, rocky terrain that requires careful footing.
